title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a journalist called Daniel talking about a reporting scheme for young people. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a journalist called Daniel talking about a reporting scheme for young people. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A journalist called Daniel introduces a reporting scheme for young people.
extracts.audio_script: Hello. I’m Daniel and I edit Town Voice, a local online newspaper. We have recently introduced a scheme giving young people the chance to learn about reporting and produce real stories. Our organisation no longer prints daily newspapers, but our office still has a connection with publishing: the building used to be a [9]printing workshop[/9], and a few of the old machines remain on display in the entrance. Young reporters meet there once a week after school and are supported by professional journalists and university students.||The website covers local business, culture, sport and community news. We asked new applicants which subjects they were most interested in covering. I assumed music reviews would come top, but by far the most requested section was our page about [10]environmental issues[/10], including recycling, public transport and green spaces. At the first training meeting, students are not sent out to find stories. Instead, they learn how to prepare [11]interview questions[/11], avoiding questions that can be answered only with yes or no and practising how to listen to what people say.||There is some useful equipment available at the office. Students can borrow cameras for planned assignments, although their phones are often sufficient for photographs. Everybody is issued with a small [12]voice recorder[/12] when interviewing people, after asking permission, so quotes can be checked accurately later. The first group assignment is a report about local [13]market traders[/13], whose stalls provide food, crafts and clothing in the town square. Each student will speak to one stallholder and then work together on a single article describing the market's role in the community.||All finished articles are checked by an editor before being published. At first, we intended to create a separate printed magazine for young reporters, but printing was too expensive and difficult to update. Their work will therefore appear in a special [14]online edition[/14] of Town Voice each month. Students can also choose one extra workshop. Last term, video reporting attracted only a small number. This term, applications are already highest for [15]photography training[/15], led by one of our staff photographers, who will show how to choose images that inform readers without invading privacy.||Young reporters are treated professionally. When attending organised events, they will carry a [16]press pass[/16], clearly showing that they are working for Town Voice. Students who complete the scheme and publish at least three pieces may ask for a [17]reference[/17] when applying for further study or work experience. Our final project this year will focus on a community campaign. The town’s [18]youth centre[/18] needs improved computer facilities, so students will write about its activities and help advertise a fundraising evening. Applications close next Friday. Anyone interested should complete the form online and include a short paragraph explaining which local story they would most like to report.

dialogue.speaker: daniel
dialogue.text: Hello. I’m Daniel and I edit Town Voice, a local online newspaper. We have recently introduced a scheme giving young people the chance to learn about reporting and produce real stories. Our organisation no longer prints daily newspapers, but our office still has a connection with publishing: the building used to be a printing workshop, and a few of the old machines remain on display in the entrance. Young reporters meet there once a week after school and are supported by professional journalists and university students.||The website covers local business, culture, sport and community news. We asked new applicants which subjects they were most interested in covering. I assumed music reviews would come top, but by far the most requested section was our page about environmental issues, including recycling, public transport and green spaces. At the first training meeting, students are not sent out to find stories. Instead, they learn how to prepare interview questions, avoiding questions that can be answered only with yes or no and practising how to listen to what people say.||There is some useful equipment available at the office. Students can borrow cameras for planned assignments, although their phones are often sufficient for photographs. Everybody is issued with a small voice recorder when interviewing people, after asking permission, so quotes can be checked accurately later. The first group assignment is a report about local market traders, whose stalls provide food, crafts and clothing in the town square. Each student will speak to one stallholder and then work together on a single article describing the market's role in the community.||All finished articles are checked by an editor before being published. At first, we intended to create a separate printed magazine for young reporters, but printing was too expensive and difficult to update. Their work will therefore appear in a special online edition of Town Voice each month. Students can also choose one extra workshop. Last term, video reporting attracted only a small number. This term, applications are already highest for photography training, led by one of our staff photographers, who will show how to choose images that inform readers without invading privacy.||Young reporters are treated professionally. When attending organised events, they will carry a press pass, clearly showing that they are working for Town Voice. Students who complete the scheme and publish at least three pieces may ask for a reference when applying for further study or work experience. Our final project this year will focus on a community campaign. The town’s youth centre needs improved computer facilities, so students will write about its activities and help advertise a fundraising evening. Applications close next Friday. Anyone interested should complete the form online and include a short paragraph explaining which local story they would most like to report.
